title: Everything Is an Object
question: What does 'everything is an object' mean in Ruby?
answer: In Ruby every value—including integers, symbols, nil, true, and even classes themselves—is an object that responds to methods. There are no primitives; you can call methods directly on literals.
explanation: Because all values descend from BasicObject via Object, they share methods like class, respond_to?, and object_id. Even classes are instances of Class, which makes Ruby deeply uniform and enables powerful metaprogramming.
codeExample:
puts 42.class            # Integer
puts 42.even?            # true
puts nil.class           # NilClass
puts "hi".class.class    # Class
puts 1.methods.size > 0  # true
bestPractices: Leverage that literals respond to methods to write expressive code.
Use respond_to? to check capabilities instead of type.
commonMistakes: Assuming numbers or nil are primitives like in Java.
Forgetting that classes are objects too.
interviewTips: Show 42.class and NilClass to prove the point.
Mention that classes are instances of Class.

title: Symbols vs Strings
question: What is the difference between a symbol and a string in Ruby?
answer: A symbol (:name) is an immutable, reusable identifier stored once in memory, while a string is a mutable sequence of characters. Symbols with the same name are always the same object; strings with the same text are usually distinct objects.
explanation: Symbols are ideal for hash keys, method names, and constant identifiers because comparing them is fast and they are interned. Strings are better for text you manipulate or display.
codeExample:
puts :name.object_id == :name.object_id   # true
puts "name".object_id == "name".object_id # false

user = { name: "Ada", role: :admin }
puts user[:name]                          # Ada
puts :hello.to_s                           # hello
puts "hello".to_sym.inspect                # :hello
bestPractices: Use symbols for hash keys and identifiers.
Use strings for user-facing or mutable text.
commonMistakes: Creating many dynamic symbols from user input (memory concerns pre-2.2).
Mixing symbol and string keys in the same hash.
interviewTips: Show object_id equality to prove symbols are interned.
Mention to_sym / to_s conversions.

title: Blocks, Procs, and Lambdas
question: What are the differences between blocks, procs, and lambdas?
answer: A block is an anonymous chunk of code passed to a method (not an object); a Proc is a block captured as an object; a lambda is a special Proc with strict argument checking and a return that only exits the lambda. Blocks are the most common; procs and lambdas let you store and pass callables.
explanation: Lambdas check arity (raising on wrong argument count) and treat return like a normal method return, whereas procs ignore extra/missing args and a return inside a proc returns from the enclosing method. This distinction matters for control flow.
codeExample:
square = ->(x) { x * x }        # lambda
puts square.call(4)              # 16

double = Proc.new { |x| x * 2 } # proc
puts double.call(5)             # 10

def run
  l = -> { return 1 }
  l.call                          # returns from lambda only
  2
end
puts run                         # 2
bestPractices: Use lambdas when you want strict arity and local return.
Prefer blocks for simple iteration.
commonMistakes: Expecting a proc's return to exit only the proc.
Passing the wrong number of args to a lambda.
interviewTips: Explain arity checking and return semantics differences.
Show lambda? returning true/false to distinguish them.

title: The yield Keyword
question: How does yield work in Ruby?
answer: yield invokes the block passed to the current method, optionally passing arguments to it. It lets a method hand control back to caller-supplied code, forming the basis of Ruby's iterator pattern.
explanation: Any method can accept an implicit block and call it with yield. block_given? checks whether a block was provided, avoiding a LocalJumpError. This enables flexible APIs like each, tap, and custom resource wrappers.
codeExample:
def repeat(n)
  return enum_for(:repeat, n) unless block_given?
  i = 0
  while i < n
    yield i
    i += 1
  end
end

repeat(3) { |i| puts "iteration #{i}" }
bestPractices: Guard yield with block_given? to avoid LocalJumpError.
Return an Enumerator when no block is given for chainability.
commonMistakes: Calling yield without a block passed.
Forgetting to pass values that the block expects.
interviewTips: Show block_given? and enum_for pattern.
Explain how each is implemented with yield.

title: method_missing & Metaprogramming
question: What is method_missing and how is it used in metaprogramming?
answer: method_missing is a hook Ruby calls when an object receives a message it has no method for. Overriding it lets you handle dynamic method names at runtime—the foundation of dynamic proxies and DSLs like ActiveRecord's dynamic finders.
explanation: You should pair method_missing with respond_to_missing? so the object honestly reports which dynamic methods it supports (affecting respond_to? and method lookup). Overuse hurts performance and debuggability, so define real methods when the set is known.
bestPractices: Always call super for unhandled names.
Implement respond_to_missing? alongside method_missing.
commonMistakes: Forgetting super, so real NoMethodError never surfaces.
Using method_missing where define_method would be clearer/faster.
interviewTips: Mention define_method as an alternative.
Explain why respond_to_missing? matters.

title: Modules and Mixins
question: How do include, extend, and prepend differ when mixing in modules?
answer: include adds a module's methods as instance methods; extend adds them as singleton (class or object) methods; prepend inserts the module before the class in the ancestor chain so its methods override and can super into the class's own methods.
explanation: Ruby uses single inheritance but achieves composition through mixins. The three keywords control where the module sits in the method resolution order (ancestors), which determines override behaviour—prepend is especially useful for wrapping existing methods.
codeExample:
module Greet
  def hello = "hi from #{self.class}"
end

class User
  include Greet
end
puts User.new.hello           # instance method

obj = Object.new
obj.extend(Greet)
puts obj.hello                # singleton method

module Loud
  def hello = super.upcase
end
class User
  prepend Loud
end
puts User.new.hello           # HI FROM USER
bestPractices: Use prepend to wrap/override existing methods with super.
Use extend to add class-level methods from a module.
commonMistakes: Confusing include (instance) with extend (singleton).
Expecting include to override the class's own method.
interviewTips: Show the ancestors array to explain resolution order.
Explain a real prepend use case (instrumentation).

title: Duck Typing
question: What is duck typing in Ruby?
answer: Duck typing means an object's suitability is determined by the methods it responds to, not its class—'if it walks like a duck and quacks like a duck, it's a duck.' Ruby code depends on behaviour (interface) rather than explicit types.
explanation: Instead of checking is_a?, idiomatic Ruby checks respond_to? or simply calls the method and lets any conforming object work. This encourages flexible, polymorphic code but requires clear conventions and good tests since there is no compile-time type check.
codeExample:
def print_length(obj)
  raise ArgumentError, "needs #length" unless obj.respond_to?(:length)
  puts obj.length
end

print_length("hello")   # 5
print_length([1, 2, 3]) # 3
print_length({ a: 1 })  # 1
bestPractices: Program to behaviour (respond_to?) not class.
Document the informal interface a method expects.
commonMistakes: Overusing is_a?/kind_of? checks that defeat polymorphism.
Assuming duck typing removes the need for tests.
interviewTips: Contrast duck typing with static type checking.
Mention respond_to? as the runtime check.

title: attr_accessor and Friends
question: What do attr_accessor, attr_reader, and attr_writer do?
answer: They are metaprogramming helpers that generate getter and/or setter methods for instance variables. attr_reader creates a getter, attr_writer a setter, and attr_accessor both, eliminating boilerplate accessor definitions.
explanation: Instance variables (@name) are private to the object; you expose them through methods. These macros define those methods at class-definition time, keeping classes concise while preserving encapsulation—you can later replace a generated accessor with custom logic transparently.
codeExample:
class User
  attr_accessor :name      # getter + setter
  attr_reader :id          # getter only

  def initialize(id, name)
    @id = id
    @name = name
  end
end

u = User.new(1, "Ada")
u.name = "Grace"
puts u.name              # Grace
puts u.id                # 1
bestPractices: Expose only the accessors you need (reader vs writer).
Replace a macro with a real method when logic is required.
commonMistakes: Using attr_accessor for everything, breaking encapsulation.
Forgetting @ when assigning inside a custom setter (creates a local).
interviewTips: Explain these are just method generators.
Show attr_reader for read-only state.

title: Method Visibility
question: How do public, private, and protected control method visibility in Ruby?
answer: public methods are callable by anyone (the default); private methods can only be called without an explicit receiver (implicitly on self); protected methods can be called with an explicit receiver but only from within the same class or subclass.
explanation: Ruby's private is about the receiver, not access location: you cannot call a private method as obj.method (with a few exceptions like private setters). protected enables collaboration between instances of the same class—useful for comparison operators that need another instance's internals.
codeExample:
class Account
  def initialize(balance) = @balance = balance

  def >(other)
    balance > other.balance      # protected call on other
  end

  protected

  def balance = @balance
end

puts(Account.new(100) > Account.new(50))  # true
bestPractices: Use protected for intra-class comparisons.
Keep helper methods private to shrink the public API.
commonMistakes: Trying to call a private method with an explicit receiver.
Confusing Ruby private with Java private (location-based).
interviewTips: Explain private is receiver-based, not location-based.
Give a protected comparison example.

title: Exception Handling
question: How does begin/rescue/ensure work in Ruby?
answer: begin wraps risky code, rescue handles specific exception classes, else runs when no exception occurred, and ensure always runs for cleanup. Methods have an implicit begin, so you can rescue at the method level too.
explanation: Ruby exceptions are objects descending from Exception, but you should rescue StandardError (the default) rather than Exception to avoid catching signals like Interrupt. retry re-runs the begin block, and raise with no args re-raises the current error.
codeExample:
def fetch(attempts = 3)
  tries = 0
  begin
    tries += 1
    risky_call
  rescue Net::TimeoutError => e
    retry if tries < attempts
    raise
  ensure
    puts "cleanup after #{tries} tries"
  end
end
bestPractices: Rescue StandardError, not Exception.
Use ensure for resource cleanup that must always run.
commonMistakes: Rescuing Exception and swallowing signals.
Using exceptions for normal control flow.
interviewTips: Explain retry and bare raise re-raising.
Mention custom exception classes inheriting StandardError.

title: Comparable and Enumerable
question: What do the Comparable and Enumerable modules provide?
answer: Comparable gives you <, <=, ==, >, >=, and between? once you define the <=> (spaceship) operator. Enumerable provides map, select, reduce, sort, and dozens more once you define each. Both are mixins that add rich behaviour from one required method.
explanation: These modules are Ruby's answer to interfaces: implement a single primitive (each or <=>) and inherit a whole family of methods. This is a textbook use of mixins and shows why so many core collection methods work uniformly across types.
codeExample:
class Version
  include Comparable
  attr_reader :n
  def initialize(n) = @n = n
  def <=>(other) = n <=> other.n
end

puts Version.new(2) > Version.new(1)   # true

class Bag
  include Enumerable
  def initialize(*items) = @items = items
  def each(&block) = @items.each(&block)
end

puts Bag.new(3, 1, 2).sort.inspect      # [1, 2, 3]
bestPractices: Define <=> for Comparable and each for Enumerable.
Return nil from <=> for incomparable objects.
commonMistakes: Forgetting to include the module after defining the primitive.
Implementing each without yielding elements.
interviewTips: Explain the single-method contract of each module.
Show map/sort appearing 'for free'.

title: Iterators: each, map, select, reduce
question: What is the difference between each, map, select, and reduce?
answer: each iterates for side effects and returns the original collection; map transforms each element into a new array; select (filter) keeps elements where the block is truthy; reduce (inject) accumulates elements into a single value. They are the core Enumerable iterators.
explanation: These methods make Ruby collection code declarative. map/select/reduce return new values without mutating the source (favouring immutability), while each is for effects like printing. Chaining them composes readable data pipelines.
codeExample:
nums = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]

nums.each { |n| print n }        # side effect: 12345
doubled = nums.map { |n| n * 2 } # [2, 4, 6, 8, 10]
evens   = nums.select(&:even?)   # [2, 4]
sum     = nums.reduce(0, :+)     # 15

puts nums.select(&:odd?).map { |n| n ** 2 }.inspect  # [1, 9, 25]
bestPractices: Use map/select/reduce for transformations, each for side effects.
Chain methods to express pipelines clearly.
commonMistakes: Using map when you mean each (creating a wasted array).
Mutating the collection while iterating.
interviewTips: Show &:even? symbol-to-proc shorthand.
Explain reduce with an initial value and symbol.

title: Truthiness in Ruby
question: What values are falsy in Ruby?
answer: Only nil and false are falsy in Ruby. Every other value—including 0, empty strings, and empty arrays—is truthy. This makes conditional logic predictable compared to languages with many falsy values.
explanation: Because 0 and "" are truthy, you must explicitly test emptiness with methods like empty? or zero?. The safe navigation operator (&.) and idioms like value || default rely on this simple two-value falsiness.
codeExample:
puts "zero truthy" if 0            # prints (0 is truthy)
puts "empty truthy" if ""          # prints ("" is truthy)
puts "nil falsy" unless nil         # prints

name = nil
puts(name || "default")             # default

user&.profile&.email                # safe navigation
bestPractices: Test emptiness with empty?/zero?, not truthiness.
Use ||= and value || default for defaults.
commonMistakes: Assuming 0 or empty string is falsy (they are truthy).
Confusing nil checks with false checks.
interviewTips: State that only nil and false are falsy.
Mention the safe navigation operator &..

title: freeze and Immutability
question: What does freeze do and why use it?
answer: freeze makes an object immutable—any attempt to modify it raises a FrozenError. It is used to protect constants, share safe data across threads, and (via frozen_string_literal) reduce string allocations for performance.
explanation: Ruby objects are mutable by default, so constants can be changed unless frozen. freeze is shallow: freezing an array does not freeze its elements. The magic comment frozen_string_literal: true freezes all string literals in a file, a common performance and safety optimization.
codeExample:
# frozen_string_literal: true

CONFIG = { host: "localhost" }.freeze
puts CONFIG.frozen?          # true
# CONFIG[:host] = "x"        # FrozenError

list = [+"a", +"b"].freeze
# list << "c"                # FrozenError
list[0] << "z"               # allowed: elements not frozen
puts list.inspect            # ["az", "b"]
bestPractices: Freeze constants to prevent accidental mutation.
Use frozen_string_literal to cut string allocations.
commonMistakes: Assuming freeze is deep (it is shallow).
Modifying a frozen object and hitting FrozenError.
interviewTips: Explain shallow vs deep freezing.
Mention the frozen_string_literal magic comment.

title: String Interpolation
question: How does string interpolation work in Ruby?
answer: Inside double-quoted strings, #{expression} evaluates any Ruby expression and inserts its to_s result. Single-quoted strings do not interpolate and treat #{} literally.
explanation: Interpolation is preferred over concatenation because it is faster and more readable and automatically calls to_s on the embedded value. Any expression—method calls, arithmetic, conditionals—can appear inside #{}.
codeExample:
name = "Ada"
age = 36
puts "#{name} is #{age} years old"          # Ada is 36 years old
puts "Next year: #{age + 1}"                  # Next year: 37
puts 'No #{interpolation} here'               # literal
puts "Upper: #{name.upcase}"                 # Upper: ADA
bestPractices: Prefer interpolation over + concatenation.
Keep interpolated expressions simple for readability.
commonMistakes: Using single quotes and expecting interpolation.
Building strings with slow repeated concatenation.
interviewTips: Contrast single vs double quotes.
Note that #{} calls to_s automatically.

title: Splat and Keyword Arguments
question: How do splat (*) and keyword (**) arguments work in Ruby?
answer: A single splat (*args) collects extra positional arguments into an array; a double splat (**opts) collects extra keyword arguments into a hash. Keyword arguments can be required or have defaults, making method signatures explicit and flexible.
explanation: Splats also work for spreading: *arr expands an array into arguments and **hash expands a hash into keywords. Since Ruby 3.0 positional and keyword arguments are strictly separated, so a trailing hash is no longer auto-converted to keywords.
codeExample:
def event(name, *tags, priority: :low, **meta)
  puts "#{name} #{tags.inspect} #{priority} #{meta.inspect}"
end

event("deploy", "ci", "prod", priority: :high, user: "ada")
# deploy ["ci", "prod"] high {:user=>"ada"}

args = [1, 2, 3]
puts [*args, 4].inspect        # [1, 2, 3, 4]
bestPractices: Use keyword args for clarity when methods have many options.
Use * and ** to forward arguments transparently.
commonMistakes: Relying on Ruby 2 hash-to-keyword auto-conversion (removed in 3.0).
Mixing up * (array) and ** (hash) splats.
interviewTips: Show argument forwarding with (*args, **kwargs, &block).
Mention the Ruby 3.0 separation change.

title: Ranges and Method Chaining
question: How do ranges and method chaining work in Ruby?
answer: A range (1..10 inclusive, 1...10 exclusive) represents an interval and is Enumerable, so it supports iteration and conversion. Method chaining links calls together because most methods return an object, enabling fluent, readable pipelines.
explanation: Ranges support numbers, characters, and endless/beginless forms (1.., ..5). Chaining works because Enumerable methods return new collections; tap and then (yield_self) help insert side effects or transformations without breaking a chain.
codeExample:
puts (1..5).to_a.inspect            # [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
puts ('a'..'e').to_a.inspect        # ["a", "b", "c", "d", "e"]
puts (1..100).step(10).to_a.inspect # [1, 11, 21, ...]

result = (1..20)
  .select(&:even?)
  .map { |n| n * n }
  .reduce(:+)
puts result                         # 1540
bestPractices: Use exclusive ranges (...) for zero-based lengths.
Chain Enumerable methods for readable pipelines.
commonMistakes: Confusing inclusive (..) with exclusive (...) ranges.
Creating huge intermediate arrays instead of lazy enumeration.
interviewTips: Mention endless ranges (1..) and .lazy.
Show tap for debugging inside a chain.

title: ObjectSpace and GC Basics
question: What are ObjectSpace and Ruby's garbage collector?
answer: ObjectSpace lets you introspect all live objects (iterate them, count by class, define finalizers). Ruby's GC automatically reclaims unreachable objects using a generational, incremental mark-and-sweep collector (in MRI).
explanation: MRI's GC groups objects into young and old generations, collecting the young frequently and old rarely for efficiency. You can tune or trigger it via the GC module (GC.start, GC.stat), while ObjectSpace.each_object and count_objects aid debugging and memory profiling.
codeExample:
puts ObjectSpace.count_objects[:T_STRING] > 0   # true

count = ObjectSpace.each_object(String) { }
puts "live strings: #{count}"

GC.start                        # force a collection
puts GC.stat(:count)           # number of GC runs
bestPractices: Use ObjectSpace and GC.stat for memory profiling, not production logic.
Let the GC run automatically; tune only with evidence.
commonMistakes: Relying on ObjectSpace.each_object in performance-critical code.
Assuming GC.start is needed routinely.
interviewTips: Describe generational mark-and-sweep.
Mention GC.stat and count_objects for diagnostics.

title: Singleton Methods
question: What are singleton methods in Ruby?
answer: A singleton method is a method defined on a single specific object rather than on its class, so only that object responds to it. Class methods (def self.foo) are actually singleton methods on the class object itself.
explanation: Singleton methods live in the object's singleton class (accessible via singleton_class or class << self). This mechanism underlies class methods, per-object customization, and extend, and it inserts a hidden class into the object's ancestor lookup.
codeExample:
user = Object.new
def user.greet = "hello!"
puts user.greet             # hello!

other = Object.new
# other.greet               # NoMethodError

class Service
  class << self
    def call = "class-level singleton"
  end
end
puts Service.call           # class-level singleton
bestPractices: Use class << self to group several class methods.
Reserve per-object methods for genuine special cases.
commonMistakes: Expecting other instances to gain a singleton method.
Confusing singleton methods with the Singleton design pattern.
interviewTips: Explain that def self.x defines a singleton method.
Mention the singleton (eigen) class.

title: Constants, Class vs Instance Variables
question: How do constants, class variables, and instance variables differ in Ruby?
answer: Constants start with an uppercase letter and are scoped to their class/module (mutable but warn on reassignment). Class variables (@@count) are shared across a class and all subclasses. Instance variables (@name) belong to a single object. Class instance variables (@x at class level) belong to the class object itself and are not shared with subclasses.
explanation: @@ class variables are notorious because they are shared down the inheritance tree, causing surprises in subclasses; many prefer class instance variables instead. Constants provide namespaced, semi-immutable values and can be referenced with the :: scope operator.
codeExample:
class Counter
  MAX = 100                 # constant
  @@total = 0               # class variable (shared w/ subclasses)
  @instances = 0            # class instance variable

  def initialize
    @id = (@@total += 1)    # instance variable
  end

  def self.total = @@total
end

Counter.new; Counter.new
puts Counter.total          # 2
puts Counter::MAX           # 100
bestPractices: Prefer class instance variables over @@ for per-class state.
Freeze mutable constants to prevent modification.
commonMistakes: Using @@ variables and being surprised by subclass sharing.
Reassigning a constant (Ruby only warns, does not error).
interviewTips: Contrast @@ (shared) with class instance variables (not shared).
Mention the :: constant scoping operator.
